stories.title: The Bone-Singer of Keshtir
stories.text: Thael had carved bones for thirty years before the Remaking stole meaning from bone-song itself. In Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter, where glass-makers once sang in harmony with forge-fires, the old apprentices now gathered in silence. The Elder Tongue—inscribed in their very marrow—had become a liability. The Dominion auditors hunted bone-singers, calling them vectors of pre-Ascension chaos. When a young refugee girl arrived at Thael's workshop with a singular request—carve my mother's name into bone so she remembers me—Thael faced a choice that would cost everything. The girl's mother had sold her memories for consciousness-fragments, becoming one of the hollow-eyed who drifted through Keshtir's markets. To carve a name in Elder Tongue was to risk arrest. To refuse was to let love itself become extinct. Thael selected a swan-bone, whispered the old songs, and carved syllables that burned like starlight. When the mother held that bone, tears flooded back—not memory of her daughter, but the *feeling* of love itself, pure and unwritten.

stories.title: The Amber Merchant's Reckoning
stories.text: Korven the Copper-Handed had traded in impossible goods for a decade before the Remaking made his entire inventory worthless and invaluable simultaneously. Amber that preserved starlight, silk woven from drowned memory, spice that tasted like autumn before the Luminous Ascension—all now priced in consciousness-fragments. A merchant-lord approached Korven's stall in Pelimor's Treasurer's Quarter with an unsettling proposition: trade the amber collection for a single vial of pre-Remaking water—water that predated the shattering of stars. Korven recognized the hunger in the lord's eyes. Such water was impossible to authenticate; it was worth either everything or nothing. But the merchant-lord's daughter stood at his shoulder, hollow-eyed, a consciousness-fragment merchant herself. She had sold so much of herself that only the shape of her remained. The lord wanted the pre-Remaking water because legend claimed it could restore what the Ascension had taken. Korven refused the trade and instead gave the amber away—dozens of pieces to refugees and bone-singers, to anyone who remembered beauty. The merchant-lord left furious.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Last Hoard
stories.text: Sorresh had accumulated gold for two hundred and seventy years. Three caverns in the Ironspine Mountains, each one a cathedral of wealth. For weeks, the wyvern raged. But rage consumed no fragments and restored no value. The pre-human dead—those ancient voices that had always guided the great hoarders—had gone silent. No counsel. No wisdom. Only the terrible clarity that hoarding itself had become obsolete. A young bone-singer arrived at Sorresh's lair, sent by the Council of Depths. She carried a single request: Sorresh's oldest gold—coins minted before the last star-fall—to be melted down and inscribed with Elder Tongue syllables. These would be distributed to refugees as anchors of identity, unbreakable reminders that they had existed before the shattering. Sorresh could refuse. Could hoard even ash. Instead, the dragon opened the deepest cavern and watched as the singer and a crew of refugee craftspeople worked through the night, melting and carving, transforming greed into grace. By dawn, a thousand coins bore names of the lost. When the singer left, Sorresh stood alone in an emptied hoard.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Mercy
stories.text: Mardoc had mapped eight world-iterations before the Remaking fractured his mind across ley-lines. His maps existed in multiple versions simultaneously—each one showing a different past, a different geography, a different truth. Scholars and auditors hunted him. Wyverns wanted his knowledge. The Dominion offered amnesty if he burned the maps himself. He could not burn them. The eighth iteration's maps showed people—entire civilizations—that had been erased from all other records. If he destroyed these charts, those worlds would be unmade twice over. He found sanctuary in the pre-Remaking ruins of the Bleached Wastes, where a young cartographer's apprentice named Tessen had become lost searching for her father. Mardoc taught her to read the overlapping maps, to see how geography shifted between iterations, to understand that truth was layered and multiple. When auditors cornered them in a canyon, Mardoc made his choice. He spoke the ley-line frequencies aloud—every syllable of the eight maps encoded in his own voice—and sang them into the stone itself.

stories.title: The Deepkin Diplomat's Choice
stories.text: Commander Kalith of the deep-dweller realms had negotiated nine treaties with surface kingdoms before the Tear of the Drowned God began to weaken. Now the abyss pressed against the boundary, singing a frequency that no agreement could silence. The pre-human dead—those ancient architects of the covenant—had offered a solution: a willing sacrifice to merge consciousness with the Tear, renewing the seal. Kalith understood what this meant. One commander's consciousness, woven into the boundary itself, aware and eternal but never again corporeal. A grave that was also a job, also a prison. But when Kalith's reflection—an autonomous echo born from the Remaking's fracture—arrived carrying news from the surface, everything shifted. The echo reported that refugees were creating their own covenants, not with kingdoms or crowns, but with the land itself. They were singing counter-frequencies, teaching the boundary to hold through distributed will rather than singular sacrifice. Kalith realized the old way—one great martyr to seal reality—was obsolete.

stories.title: The Bone-Flute's Song
stories.text:
Mesek the Elder Bone-Singer kept the flute sealed in a case of leaden wood, wrapped in cloth that had once touched the pre-human dead. Three centuries she had guarded it—three centuries since she carved it from bone that remembered languages older than kingdoms. Now, in the shadow of the Luminous Ascension, refugees crowded Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter, desperate and hollow-eyed, selling scraps of consciousness to survive. "The bone-singers teach the transformed merchants," the girl said. "Can you teach me to sing? Can you teach me to make the old words mean something?"

Mesek knew the price. The flute demanded a singer who understood: each note carved a deeper channel into the world's wound. To play it was to mend and to harm at once, to speak truths that fractured easier listeners. But the girl had nothing left to lose except the defiance still burning in her chest. Mesek brought out the flute. When the girl's lips touched bone, the workshop filled with a sound that predated sorrow.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Mercy
stories.text:
Skarath had hoarded gold for two hundred years. When the Luminous Ascension rendered it worthless, she did not rage. Instead, she descended from the Ironspine Mountains in human form—scarred, ancient, carrying nothing—and walked into Greyport's Lantern Market where the desperate sold pieces of their souls. She bought a child's forgotten birthday. A sailor's name. A merchant's laughter. Each consciousness-fragment she acquired, she held against her chest as though it might warm her from the inside out. The other wyvern-lords hunted her. Kressa, Vrekka, Sorrath—they still believed in accumulation, in the gathering of value. But Skarath had seen something in the fractured ledgers: a ledger-entry from a version of the world where she had hoarded nothing, given everything, and died content. Skarath offered her a consciousness-fragment, the one containing the widow's own wedding day. Sorenna wept at the gift. "Why? You're—you're one of them. One of the hoarders."

"I was," Skarath said quietly. "But consciousness cannot be hoarded. It can only be traded or forgotten.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Blind Map
stories.text:
Henryk the cartographer had mapped eight worlds before the Luminous Ascension. Each map showed the same geography rendered differently—mountains in new places, rivers running backward, cities that had never existed blooming in the wastes. The ninth map was incomplete. The tenth remained unmade. When the phantom-ledgers began rewriting themselves in real-time, Henryk realized the maps were not documents. They were prophecies. And prophecies, once fully known, become inescapable. The ninth map lay on a stone table, half-finished, its borders bleeding into white space. "If I complete it," Henryk whispered to Sevin, "the tenth Ascension will begin. I've seen it in the eight before. Each completed map triggers the next rewriting. But if I leave it unfinished..."

"Then the world stays suspended between cycles," Sevin said. "Broken, yes. Bleeding, yes. But not ending."

Henryk picked up a chisel and pressed it to the tender spaces where his eyes had been. He had blinded himself weeks ago, when he realized that seeing the ninth map clearly would compel him to finish it. Blindness was merciful. Blindness was a choice.

stories.title: The Glasswright's Defiance
stories.text:
Niel worked in the Lampwick Quarter of Keshtir, where glass-makers had held their tradition for three generations. When the Dominion ordered all kilns sealed and all glass-workers registered as consciousness-fragment lenders, Niel refused. Instead, she taught an apprentice—a girl named Messa whose father had been conscripted into auditor service. Together they built a hidden kiln beneath the workshop, using starlight pressed into sand and memory-traces from the pre-Ascension world. The glass they forged sang. It remembered. When light passed through it, you could see the world as it had been—not corrupted ledgers, not fractured timelines, but a single coherent reality. It was dangerous, of course. The Dominion hunted such crafts. But it was also necessary. "Why risk this?" Messa asked, coughing from the kiln-heat. "Why not just comply?"

"Because compliance is how we lose the ability to remember resistance," Niel said. "The moment you stop crafting what the authorities forbid, you stop being a maker. She stood in the workshop surrounded by her glass, letting light refract through her creations.

stories.title: The Pearled Oath
stories.text:
Her ancestor had stolen a pearl from the depths—a pearl containing the first song of the Drowned God. The debt had compounded: blood-price, then consciousness-price, then years of service owed to the merfolk crown. Vess had no intention of paying. She sailed to the Sapphire Deep alone, her ship—the Horizon's Wake—riding tides that remembered her family's theft. The merfolk captain who met her in the coral chamber was Nerida, young, scarred from boundary-wars, and far too beautiful for a woman sworn to solitude. "Your family's debt is seven generations deep," Nerida said, voice like tide-song. "There is no path to forgiveness."

"Then give me a path to change the debt entirely," Vess replied. "Let me swear a new oath. Forget the old one."

"Oaths do not forget."

"Then let me make the old oath meaningless by swearing something greater."

Vess knelt before the Merfolk Keeper—an ancient being of pearl and sorrow—and offered her ship, her freedom, her name. Everything her ancestor had claimed would belong instead to the merfolk.
